title: SSH Brute-Force (Single User, Wordlist)
description: Attempts to brute-force the SSH password for a known username by iterating through a provided wordlist. Hydra is exceptionally fast but easily detected by defensive tools like Fail2Ban.
when_to_use: Use this when you have enumerated a valid username on the target (e.g., 'root' or 'admin') and suspect they might be using a weak or default password.
common_pitfalls: Default thread counts might crash older SSH servers or trigger aggressive lockout policies immediately.

optional_flags.description: Number of parallel connects per target

title: HTTP POST Form Brute-Force
description: Targeting a web application's login form. You must provide the exact HTTP path, the structure of the POST body (with ^USER^ and ^PASS^ as placeholders), and a string that appears only when the login fails.
when_to_use: Essential for brute-forcing custom web application logins, WordPress admin panels, or internal portals that do not implement rate limiting or CAPTCHAs.
common_pitfalls: Getting the failure string wrong will result in 100% false positives, where Hydra thinks every password worked. Always manually test a failed login first.

optional_flags.description: Number of parallel connects per target

title: Automated Hash Cracking (Wordlist)
description: The fundamental command for offline cracking. John the Ripper attempts to automatically identify the hash type in the file and uses the provided wordlist to crack it.
when_to_use: Your first step after extracting password hashes (e.g., from an /etc/shadow file or a database dump). If you aren't sure what hash type it is, John is usually very good at guessing.

title: Show Cracked Passwords
description: Displays the plaintext passwords that have already been successfully cracked and saved in John's internal potfile.
when_to_use: Run this after a long cracking session finishes to clearly view the results mapped to their original usernames or identifiers.

title: Crack MD5 Hashes (Wordlist Mode)
description: Uses Hashcat's immense GPU cracking power to brute-force MD5 hashes (mode 0) using a straight wordlist attack (attack mode 0).
when_to_use: When you have a large number of basic MD5 hashes and want to utilize your computer's GPU for maximum cracking speed, which John the Ripper (CPU-focused) cannot match.

title: Crack Windows NTLM Hashes
description: Targets the NTLM hash algorithm (mode 1000) used locally by Windows and across Active Directory domains. This is one of the most common and fastest hashes to crack.
when_to_use: Immediately after dumping the local SAM database from a Windows machine or extracting the NTDS.dit file from a Domain Controller.
